svg.text: 9.7 · The durability chain — where "durable" really means, where fsync LIES
"written to disk" is a chain of layers · data can be lost (volatile) at any · fsync forces it down · but layers can lie
1 · APP — InnoDB log buffer (process RAM)
VOLATILE — crash loses anything not pushed down
⚠ volatile
2 · OS PAGE CACHE (kernel RAM) — write() lands HERE
write() returning = "kernel has it", NOT "on disk" · power loss loses it
⚠ volatile
3 · DRIVE / CONTROLLER WRITE CACHE (DRAM on the device)
⚠️ THE LYING-DISK PROBLEM: a drive may ACK fsync while data is still in THIS volatile cache
→ power loss AFTER fsync returned still loses the "durable" data
⚠ may LIE
4 · PHYSICAL PLATTER / NAND — the ONLY truly non-volatile layer ✓
data is durable ONLY once it reaches here
✓ durable
fsync forces ↓
"I called fsync, it returned" can be FALSE — durability is only as good as the last HONEST layer
● an acknowledgment is only as trustworthy as the weakest layer that LIED (acked early from volatile cache)
● for TRUE durability: drive write cache DISABLED, or NON-VOLATILE (battery/capacitor-backed), + honest fsync semantics
● cloud/network storage (EBS…) adds MORE layers + its own fsync semantics → "fast" ≠ durable; verify its guarantees
● this is HOW a "confirmed" payment is silently lost on power loss → the M15 data-loss territory (money-never-lies)
universal: OS cache · drive cache · RAID cache · network-storage cache · replication acks — ALL sit between "wrote it" and "survives power loss"
